title: RA.968 Barry Can't Swim
tracklist:
Scott Orr - Scott
Barry Can't Swim - [Unreleased]
Leon Vynehall - From The Sea/It Looms (Chapters I & II)
This Mortal Coil - The Lacemaker
Pepe - Act III. "Compact City Dream"
Stars Of The Lid - Dopamine Clouds Over Craven Cottage
Ian William Craig - Before Meaning Comes
Suso Saiz - Floating Into the Avalanche
Factory Floor - How You Say (Daniel Avery Remix)
Anthony Naples - Refugio
Hofuku Sochi - Demain (feat. Benjamin Wild & Miss Alaska)
Space Dimension Controller - 2257AD
Lorenzo Senni - Elegant, and Never Tiring
Linkwood - Nectarine
Kenji Kihara - Last Night Dream
Barry Can't Swim - [Unreleased]
Slow Attack Ensemble - Form and F a d e
Chino Yoshio - I Heard Love Has No Limits
Ryuichi Sakamoto - Ieta

translation.content: As far as crossover electronic success goes, it's fair to say Barry Can't Swim's 2024 scorecard would take some beating. Nominated for a Mercury Prize? Check. Topping Warehouse Project as a DJ and selling out multiple nights at Brixton Academy performing live? Check. Ramming Glastonbury with a show so busy, it provided one of the festival's increasingly common tells for which artists might be "next up" in the public eye? Check. Barely a year on from the release of his debut album, When Will We Land?, Barry—AKA Josh Mannie—is sitting pretty as one of the most in-demand acts working in dance and electronic music today. A star forged in the Bonobo mould, his string-accented, soft-cresting euphoria has proved irresistible for a huge swathe of listeners, spurred on by the peak-time pump he serves up alongside fellow star of the scene, salute. For RA.968, Mannie pulls in the complete opposite direction. Sure, there are nods to Mark Leckey and late-night jazz haunts throughout his catalogue, and the ruminative clouds drifting across his signature golden-hour glow do suggest an artist with a sharp grasp on texture, tone and meteorological melancholy. But a beatless collage featuring Suso Sáiz, Slow Attack Ensemble and Lorenzo Senni? An undeniable surprise, and a welcome one at that. Speckled with upfront airings of new ambient material, Barry Can't Swim's RA Podcast charts a path from This Mortal Coil to Ryuichi Sakamoto, detouring through some Linkwood and Anthony Naples cuts we've not heard for a good while. (He even includes a Stars of the Lid favourite that namechecks Fulham's home ground, an act of mid-table grace for the diehard Everton fan). RA.968 is as crisp as a frosty night walk—a holiday gift from one of the most popular acts in the game. 'Tis the season. – Gabriel Szatan How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? I've been writing a lot of music this year, a lot of ambient stuff in particular. I wanted to use this as an opportunity to share some of that, really, and recorded it in my flat in London. It's more a collage of music that has inspired my writing this year than a mix in the classic sense. You've had a 2024 full of highlights: what have been some moments that meant the most to you? Thanks! The last show I played was my own curated Warehouse Project takeover, that was such an honour and a lot of fun. Just getting to choose all your favourite artists and put them together in one space for the night was such a privilege. Is there a particular club, party or record that had a major impact on you as an artist? Growing up in Edinburgh, I spent a fairly unhealthy amount of time at Sneaky Pete's, haha. It's like a 90-capacity venue but they get amazing bookings. I've seen Erol Alkan, Machinedrum and Daniel Avery all play there to name a few. It's an institution really, I was there at least three nights a week for a good few years. The city is lucky to have it. Part of your rise is linked to the fact that you can switch seamlessly between live performances and DJ sets. Do you get the same level of enjoyment out of both? And do you feel each experience is compatible with the other? Definitely. Whenever I'm playing live a lot I'm always itching to DJ, and same vice versa. Want what you can't have, haha. They are very different though: the live show is 100 percent my own music, so it's a deeper expression and focusing on details in the production and performance. DJing is more spontaneous and unplanned. I love having the balance of both: pushing the sounds I would play in a DJ set—depending on the crowd in front of me—then taking that experience back as a learning point for the live show. What on the cards are you looking forward to next year? This year's been incredible but it can all change in a second so I try not to attach too much [weight] to any of that. I really just want to keep writing music that I love more than anything, I feel very lucky to be able to do that. I've got an album almost finished that should come out next year. And some amazing shows to be announced. Just finally, as we wave goodbye to 2024: what's the most enjoyable way you've ever rung in the new year? And the biggest dud? I never really make much effort with New Year, because it always feels like forced fun but a few years ago me and some of my boys took a camper van around the Highlands and just had a big party parked up beside a random lake, it was proper funny. That was a definite highlight. Low point: I'm pretty sure I remember getting kicked out a casino or something at like 4 AM when I was 19 and getting a lonely chippy on the way home while all my mates were still in there. Grim times.
translation.blurb: A wintry collage from one of 2024's breakout stars.
